content.problemStatement:
Given an array of **N** numbers, a pair of numbers is called good if difference between the two numbers is strictly less than **D**.

Find out maximum possible sum of all good disjoint pairs that can be made from these numbers.

Sum of X pairs is the sum of all 2*X numbers in the pairs.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and D. The second line contains N space‑separated integers representing the array.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible sum of all numbers that belong to good disjoint pairs.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: After sorting (3,5,8) only pair (3,5) satisfies diff<3, sum=8.
Test case 2: Sorted (5,8,10,12). Best is pair (10,12) sum=22.
Test case 3: Sorted (2,3,8,15,17). Pairs (2,3) and (15,17) give 5+32=37.
The only pair (10,12) has difference 2<5, so both numbers are taken, sum=22.
With only one element no pair can be formed, so the answer is 0.

solution.approach: Sort the array. After sorting, any good pair must consist of two adjacent elements because if a[i] and a[j] (i<j) satisfy a[j]-a[i] < D, then all elements between i and j also lie within that range, and pairing non‑adjacent elements would waste potential pairs. Hence we can process the sorted array from left to right using DP (or a greedy two‑pointer scan). Let dp[i] be the maximum sum using the first i elements (1‑based). For each position i (i>=2) we either skip element i (dp[i]=dp[i-1]) or, if a[i-1]-a[i-2] < D, pair them and add their sum to dp[i-2]. The answer is dp[N]. This runs in O(N log N) for sorting and O(N) for DP per test case, with O(N) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (dominated by sorting).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) auxiliary space for the DP array.
